typically when you test 'delegation' you do something like this

Say you are testing a method like this:

Object myMethod(input1) {
  :
  return delegate.doSomething(input2)

In Test

given:
  mockDelegate = Mock(Delegate)
when:
  result = objectUndertTest.myMethod('some input')
then:
  1 * mockDelagate.doSomething(expectedInput2) >> mockOutput    // could be another mock if needed, or just a simple sample object
and:
  assert result == mockOutput    


Actually when the result depend on strict mocked behavior there is NO need to check the dependent method has bene calls and in such case it is better (aslo more naturel) to write the test like so:

given:
  mockDelegate = Mock(Delegate)
  mockDelagate.doSomething(expectedInput2) >> mockOutput 
when:
  result = objectUndertTest.myMethod('some input')
then:
  assert result == mockOutput    

The fact you get 'mock' result means the method has bene called (at last once, only very sometimes in optimization bugs it might be need ot check it was not called more then once)

try to be stricter ie assert (some content) of the parameter passed here

Lombok some annotations that combine multiple other annotations (like @Data combines @ToString, @EqualsAndHashCode, @Getter / @Setter, and @RequiredArgsConstructor), here we should not “pollute” our code with too many annotations.
Duplicated string literals make the process of refactoring error-prone, since we must be sure to update all occurrences.
